soil_types: Well-drained soil rich in organic matter

pests.name: Aulacophora beetle
pests.impact: Elongated beetle about 5mm long, like a firefly. Species with orange, brown, and black colors exist. Especially damages young plants by eating holes in leaves. Rarely causes economic damage to well-grown plants. Larval stage lives in the soil and damages plant roots.
pests.solution: Destroy old crop residues, start new crops periodically, cover crops up to about 2 meters at the start of cultivation, protect natural enemies, physically destroy beetles from the onset of damage, use recommended insecticides only if essential. Recommended insecticides: Chlorantraniliprole 185g/1SC (32ml/16L water every 10 days for small plants), Spinosad 25g/1SP (16ml/16L water every 10 days for small plants), Thiocyclam 50% SP (25g/10L water every 14 days for large vines).

pests.name: Epilachna beetle
pests.impact: About 4mm long, hemispherical, brown with black spots. Larva is yellow with spines. Both adult and larva scrape leaves. Eggs are pale yellow, elongated, laid in clusters of 20-40 on leaves.
pests.solution: Immediately identify and destroy adults, larvae, and pupae upon noticing damage. Uproot and destroy old crop residues immediately after harvest. Cover new crops up to about 0.5 meters high.

pests.name: Fruit fly
pests.impact: Brown fly about 8mm long with two black spots on wings. Pierces the skin of young cucurbit fruits and lays 6-12 elongated, cream-white eggs. Legless white maggots emerge and feed on the fruit pulp, causing it to rot. Fruits become irregularly shaped. Mature larvae pupate in the soil and emerge as adults. Life cycle is about one month, with about 10 generations per year. Causes significant yield loss due to rapid flight and reproduction. Spreads further through transport of damaged fruits and maintaining infested crops.
pests.solution: Destroy all infested fruits, uproot and destroy old crops after harvest, cover new crops up to 2 meters high, deep plowing, install cue-lure pheromone traps long-term to destroy male flies, cover young fruits with polythene or paper, apply recommended insecticide-mixed protein bait at the start of flowering, spray the underside of leaves at 3-4 meter intervals every 7 days in the morning, or hang bait-coated traps.

pests.name: Mites
pests.impact: Tiny, almost invisible, orange/red spherical creatures. Scrape leaf surface and feed on sap. Damage increases in hot and dry conditions. Sap feeding reduces leaf greenness and makes them brittle.
pests.solution: Destroy old crop residues before new cultivation, spray water on crops in the evening, apply recommended miticides.

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.impact: About 1mm long, white, flying insect. Nymphs are flat, green/brown scale-like. Pierces plant leaves and sucks sap. A vector for viral diseases.
pests.solution: Uproot and destroy old crop residues before starting new cultivation, cover crops, use recommended fertilizers, spray water in the evening, and use recommended insecticides.

pests.name: Aphids
pests.impact: 1-2mm long, yellow/green/brown/black. Soft, elongated/spherical bodies, live in colonies on young plant parts. Pierce plant tissues and suck sap, excrete a sugary, viscous solution from glands at the rear. Vectors for viral diseases.
pests.solution: Destroy old crop residues, cover crops, use recommended fertilizers, wipe off by hand or brush at early stages of damage, spray water in the evening, and apply recommended insecticides. Natural enemies like ladybugs, hoverfly larvae, and parasitic wasps help control aphids under natural conditions.

diseases.name: Powdery Mildew
diseases.impact: First appears as pale yellow spots on mature leaves. Spots enlarge as white, powdery mycelium grows on the leaf surface. Severely affected leaves turn brown, shrivel, and defoliate.
diseases.solution: Use recommended fungicides at early stages of disease development. Remove infected crop residues after harvest.

diseases.name: Downy Mildew
diseases.impact: Angular yellow spots delimited by leaf veins on the upper surface of leaves. Leaves become chlorotic and eventually necrotic, and host plant cells die.
diseases.solution: Use recommended fungicides at early stages of disease development. Remove infected plant parts after harvest.

diseases.name: Foot Rot / Basal Rot
diseases.impact: In early stages, plants show temporary wilting symptoms. Leaves turn yellow, wilt, and droop. Eventually, the plant dies. Vascular bundles at the base turn yellow or brown. White fungal mycelia are visible at the base of the plant.
diseases.solution: Destroy infected plants with soil. Improve drainage. Use recommended fungicides at early stages of disease development.

diseases.name: Gummy Stem Blight
diseases.impact: Lesions typically start in water-soaked areas on leaves and fruits. Early stages may show chlorotic spots, which then turn light brown and become irregularly shaped. Dark, cracked, sunken spots appear on fruits. Plants collapse at sunken, cankered areas on the stem. Stem lesions often elongate like streaks, and cracks may exude gum. Dark brown to black pycnidia can be seen on fruits, stems, or leaves.
diseases.solution: Deep plow crop residues immediately after harvest to reduce fungal survival. Use recommended fungicides at early stages of disease development.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.impact: Sudden wilting of leaves. Eventually, the entire plant wilts. Leaves turn brown and dry. Vascular discoloration (browning). Permanent wilting.
diseases.solution: Destroy infected plants with soil. Crop rotation with resistant varieties (okra, maize). Intercropping with Cruciferae crops. Avoid moving equipment from infected fields to uninfected fields.

diseases.name: Leaf Curl Virus (LCV)
diseases.impact: Upward curling of leaves, shortening of internodes, and distortion. Stunting of plants and fruit distortion.
diseases.solution: Remove infected plants and control vectors (whiteflies) using recommended insecticides.

diseases.name: Papaya Ringspot Virus (PRSV)
diseases.impact: Mottled spots on leaves. Leaf distortions. Fruit distortion and reduced fruit size. In severe infections, oily spots appear on the fruit surface.
diseases.solution: Control vectors (aphids) and remove infected plants.

diseases.name: Cucumber Mosaic Virus (CMV)
diseases.impact: Green mottling and leaf distortion. Green spots on fruits, which turn bright yellow and rot. Fruit distortion.
diseases.solution: Remove infected plants and use virus-free seeds.

diseases.name: Zucchini Yellow Mosaic Virus (ZYMV)
diseases.impact: Stunting, yellowing, chlorosis (occasionally), mottled distorted leaves (blistering). Fruit discoloration and distortion.
diseases.solution: Remove infected plants and control vectors.

diseases.name: Phytoplasma disease / Little Leaf disease
diseases.causal_agent: Transmitted by infected seeds and leafhoppers
diseases.impact: Small, thickened, and yellowish-green leaves. Shortening of internodes and stunting of plants. Chrysanthemum-like appearance. Greening of flowers.
diseases.solution: Remove infected plants and control vectors (leafhoppers) using recommended insecticides.
